Subject: The crowded race to replace Tory
Body: This Week in Politics What to know about the six leading mayoral candidates ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ THIS WEEK IN POLITICS By David Rider Steve Russell/Toronto Star The race to replace John Tory is now a crowded but focused affair. Crowded because now that registrations are closed, we know there are a record 102 mayoral candidates, their names set in virtual stone. Mail-in ballots look like a small-print page from a big city phone book. Focused because opinion polling from three different firms identify the same leading contenders: ex-police chief Mark Saunders, Coun. Josh Matlow, ex-Liberal MPP Mitzie Hunter, ex-NDP MP Olivia Chow, Coun. Brad Bradford and ex-councillor Ana Bailão. Those polls — showing Chow the frontrunner by a significant margin, usually with Saunders and Matlow next in line — likely reflect those candidates’ high name recognition. Change is likely. Now that we’re in debate season — there were three on Wednesday alone — potential voters should start to tune in and get a sense of the people and their policies, and an idea of what they could do for Toronto’s future rather than just memories of what they’ve done in the past. Debates will continue into June, when we will also see campaign signs sprout across the city like oversized dandelions. Leading campaigns that have been furiously fundraising will unleash a torrent of advertising across multiple mediums to woo voters who can check a box, in advance polls, as early as June 8. Don't miss a thing from your favourite Star columnists. Always get the latest from the voices that matter most to you: pick the columnists you'd like to follow here, and you’ll get an email every time they have a new article online. With one month until most people vote on June 26, here is a subjective look at each of the front-runners, how they are positioning themselves and the challenges they face. Ana Bailão: Council’s former housing advocate inherited much of Tory’s former team. She has raised the most money the quickest and will advertise heavily. She needs a boost in name recognition because polling numbers often place her fourth or fifth. She has positioned herself as a housing expert who can get Premier Doug Ford to upload Toronto’s highways. Ford, however, shot that idea down. With the electorate seemingly in the mood for change, Bailão’s Tory ties might not help her. Brad Bradford: Council’s planning and housing chair is also a former Tory ally fighting to get name recognition and stand out from the pack. He has positioned himself as a “strong mayor of action” poised to blast through bureaucracy to get housing built, transit made safe and more. There’s little evidence it’s working yet, however, and his aggressive debate demeanour could turn off voters. Olivia Chow: Some political observers are surprised at her apparent lead given she hasn’t been in elected politics for eight years. Skeptics note Chow’s early lead in the 2014 mayoral election evaporated. But the vote is not far away and her positioning — an unabashedly progressive leader with a depth of experience — could catch a public mood. Her sometimes fuzzy answers, however, give an opening to rivals’ attacks that will only intensify. Mitzie Hunter: The Scarborough politician is likeable, smart and has rolled out significant policies. She would make history as Toronto’s first Black mayor. Her challenge is to raise her profile and define her progressive message — to “fix the Six” — in a way that vaults her ahead of left-leaning rivals Chow and Matlow, who are better known in civic circles. Josh Matlow: The midtown councillor is running against Ford as much as his mayoral rivals. A past Tory nemesis, he is vowing to abandon Toronto’s “get along to go along” approach to Queen’s Park and fight the interventionist premier to fix the city’s finances, build housing and more. He has lots of thoughtful policy but Chow seems to have pulled ahead as the progressive choice. Matlow’s pointed debate quips ensure he’s quoted, but won’t help dispel talk that he’s not a team player. Mark Saunders: The ex-police chief is trying to portray his lack of political experience as an asset, blaming rivals for all of Toronto’s ills that can be fixed only by his leadership. Saunders is running from the right, hitting hard on voters’ fear of crime and blaming bike lanes for aggravating traffic gridlock. His challenge is to convince voters that close ties to Ford are an asset, and rebut challenges to his record as the city’s top cop. That’s the race as it stands — but, hey, this is Toronto politics. There are 102 reasons it could all change. Here’s more of the Star’s election coverage from my colleagues: Can anyone catch Chow? Here’s what the latest polling shows. Use the Star’s promise tracker to see where the leading candidates stand on key issues. Here’s everything you need to know about how to vote. Columnist Edward Keenan writes about his takeaways and the surprising winner from Wednesday’s debates. Renters could be a force in this election, Victoria Gibson and May Warren report. But will they turn out and vote together? David Rider is city hall bureau chief for the Star. Follow him on Twitter: @dmrider. Beware of emails that ask for passwords, PINS, credit and debit card information. Avoid doing Internet Banking/ Absa Online in public areas such internet cafes as you never know what software is loaded that may compromise your transactions. Make sure no one has unauthorised access to your PC. Be especially aware that there are no security cameras trained on your PC and keyboard. Ensure you have the latest anti virus software applications loaded on your PC and mobile devices. Make sure the software on your PC is correctly licensed. Subject: RE: NorthTec Account/Password
Body: Hi Tony Not sure why it didn't work, but I manually set it to that from this end so you should be sweet to log in with that now. Regards, Kevin Jacobson ICT Services NorthTec - Tai Tokerau Wananga Private Bag 9019 Whangarei, New Zealand DDI: 09 470 3777 - Ext: 3777 FAX: 09 470 3883 -----Original Message----- From: Tony Meyer [mailto:user2.1@gvc.ceas-challenge.cc] Sent: Thursday, November 29, 2007 2:01 PM To: Kevin Jacobson Subject: Re: NorthTec Account/Password This still doesn't work. The "not used before" can't be relevant here, because this is a completely new account. In case the initial setup was triggering the "previous 24 hours", I didn't do anything with it yesterday and tried today, so that can't be relevant. The password "ABcd23." is exactly 7 characters long, has a mixture of uppercase, lowercase, and symbols, doesn't include my name, and isn't accepted. (This isn't the password I would prefer to use, but it's an example of one that doesn't work, but matches what you said). The error I get is: """ The password supplied does not meet the minimum complexity requirements. Please try again. Your password has expired and must be changed. Please change your password now. """ It seems clear that the username must be correct, otherwise it wouldn't be telling me that it has expired. Any further suggestions? Thanks, Tony On 26/11/2007, at 10:38 AM, Kevin Jacobson wrote: > Ah, sorry Tony I should have thought of that > It must be 7 chars long, with a mixture of uppercase, lowercase and > number or symbols, only requires 3 of those four. It can't be > anything you've used before, nor can it include your name. It also > can't have been changed already in the previous 24 hours, but we > shouldn't have to worry about that one. > > Regards, > Kevin Jacobson > > ICT Services > NorthTec - Tai Tokerau Wananga > Private Bag 9019 > Whangarei, New Zealand > > DDI: 09 470 3777 - Ext: 3777 > FAX: 09 470 3883 > > -----Original Message----- > From: Tony Meyer [mailto:user2.1@gvc.ceas-challenge.cc] > Sent: Monday, November 26, 2007 9:59 AM > To: Kevin Jacobson > Subject: Re: NorthTec Account/Password > > Hi Kevin, > > Thanks. When I try to change my password, I'm told that it doesn't > meet the minimum security requirements. I've tried using uppercase > +lowercase+digits+punctuation, and the maximum length it will allow, > and it still doesn't allow it. What are the minimum security > requirements? > > Cheers, > Tony > > On 26/11/2007, at 8:56 AM, Kevin Jacobson wrote: > >> Hi Tony >> Sorry I didn't get back to you last week >> Your account has been set up, your username is TAMeyer as requested >> and your password will be P@ssw0rd and your email address will be 12meyex@northtec.ac.nz >> >> After you log on for the first time you will be required to change >> your password and for your first login you will have to log into >> either our mail website athttps://mail.northland.ac.nz or any >> networked computer on campus. >> >> Regards, >> Kevin Jacobson >> >> ICT Services >> NorthTec - Tai Tokerau Wananga >> Private Bag 9019 >> Whangarei, New Zealand >> >> DDI: 09 470 3777 - Ext: 3777 >> FAX: 09 470 3883 >> > >

Subject: From Cathay's Ticket Woes to Carnival's Refresh| Grammarly + ChatGPT | Remembering Sam Zell
Body: Forbes CxO Newsletter Plus: Grammarly CEO On Why ChatGPT Won’t Kill His Business ADVERTISEMENT Anyone who's tried to book an air ticket or hotel this summer knows the pain of seeing air travel back in full force. It’s cheaper for me to fly from New York to Nairobi this summer (on points, with a 12-hour stopover in Doha both ways, but I digress) than to Europe. And my efforts to secure a ticket in Cathay Pacific’s overhyped ticket draw to Hong Kong last week found me and a dozen friends and family members trapped in a Dantesque circle of hell, not unlike a Nike SNKRS draw. When my email came in, I clicked on the link and Cathay let me into the queue. I was thrilled to see that I had an 18-minute wait instead of the hours that people complained about on social media. But when I got through, I was directed to a link that said "Book Ticket." The promised code never materialized, leaving me dinged for $USD 2630.95, which I immediately had refunded. A friend in Florida had the same thing happen to her. Nobody I know got a ticket. A Cathay spokesperson refused to confirm how many people entered the draw but I suspect the odds should make journalists feel ashamed for having showered them with so much attention. That said, I love Hong Kong and will try to soon get back there as I often do: on Japan Airlines.On a positive note, that 22-hour flight between Sydney and London is likely to be reduced to 2 hours by 2033, according to a new report. This will be achieved through sub-orbital earth-to-earth space flights. (A moment of silence to thank Jeff Bezos, Richard Branson, Elon Musk and all the other billionaires trying to leave the planet right now.) Passengers would apparently feel a g-force of 4 Gs for 30 seconds. If you’re a roller coaster junkie like me (note that the world’s tallest roller coaster is in New Jersey; South Africa’s), you know that’s on par with most rides. South Africa’s Tower of Terror even goes up to 6.3 Gs, but that’s only for a few seconds. Medical authorities are studying how much acceleration the human body can take. In the meantime, there are other ways to take a holiday a summer. The much-maligned cruise industry is having a comeback. Here’s an interview with Carnival Corporation President & CEO Josh Weinstein, who talks about the cruise line's restart and refresh since the lifting of Covid restrictions. You can also jump in a car and drive somewhere–though Florida might not be in your plans since the NAACP issued a travel advisory. When asked if this is just a stunt, as Governor Ron De Santis has charged, NAACP chair Leon Russell told my colleague, Rosemarie Miller: “That’s Bullsh*t.” He went on to explain why, as you can see here. Another friendly warning when it comes to renting cars with third-party sites. I rented a Hertz car through Priceline at the Los Angeles Airport a few weeks ago, only to have the price hiked more than $100 when I got to the counter. When I showed the agent the booking, she said it was a special code that no longer applied. Priceline later told me that was incorrect, which Hertz confirmed, and the charge was reversed. Having covered travel and aviation for The Wall Street Journal, you learn to shrug off such matters but also be vigilant. When it comes to pricing mistakes, the odds rarely seem to go in the customer's favor. But we're hitting the road for reason because there's a banquet of new experiences and places to enjoy.Safe travels! Diane Brady Assistant Managing Editor, Communities & Leadership Become a Forbes member today to unlock unlimited access to all of our stories, invitations to members-only events with industry leaders, early access to select new product launches and more. What's Next TikTok CEO Calls Montana Ban On The App Unconstitutional: Shou Zi Chew is doing everything he can to halt the ban before it goes into effect in January. Be sure to check out our excellent body of work around TikTok, Byte Dance and their business practices -- such as monitoring discussions about China, Trump and Uyghurs.The Vietnam Veteran Who Turned A 2-Bedroom House Into A $4.7 Billion Fortune: Arte Moreno, who made his first billion in billboards and now owns the L.A. Angels, sat down with Forbes to discuss his investment failures and triumphs—and why there’s no such thing as owning too much real estate.Elon Musk Tweets Support For Tim Scott Again, Raising Questions About Whether He’s Backing Away From DeSantis: Bet you're glad your CEO doesn't tweet like this. And here's why Musk's take on remote work isn't resonating, too.Philosopher Peter Singer Defends Animals, ‘Merit’ —And Courting Controversy: The influential and controversial ethicist has a new book and fresh take on cancel culture, animal rights and studying philosophy.Pussy Riot’s Nadya On Putin And Being On Russia’s Most Wanted List: “You only can require heroism from one person and this is yourself. When it comes to my family, it’s much more difficult.” Here's our interview.3 Reasons CFOs Are Well-Positioned To Support Their Organization’s ESG Success: No, it's not just because they're the keepers of the coin -- though that helps. ADVERTISEMENT By The Numbers $5.2 million That's how much Sam Zell was worth when he died at the age of 81 on May 18. Here's an interview he did with senior editor Maneet Ahuja. The CxO Profile Inside A Billionaire Bee Colony Stewart and Lynda Resnick’s Wonderful Co. is among a handful of honey bee farms responsible for the pollination of a third of Americans’ food. But the real buzz is how they rent them out. Read More → Pick from 20+ expert newsletters to stay informed on topics that you care about. Simply click on the name of the newsletter to be instantly subscribed. Subject: NedBank Account Statement 18-10-12
Body: Click here to read more Dear Customer Welcome to the movement towards a cleaner, greener, paperless world. Attached to this email is your encrypted electronic statement with enhanced security. To open it simply follow the step-by-step instructions below. Also, please advise us if your email address changes 13 that way we can ensure that you receive your eStatement safely, on time, every time. By opting for eStatements, you 19re making a tangible contribution to shrink your, and Nedbank 19s, carbon footprint. As South Africa 19s green bank, and to show our commitment to a cleaner, greener world, Nedbank will donate 25c to The Green Trust for every eStatement we send. It 19s the small things we do together that add up to make a big difference in curbing climate change. Opening your encrypted statement To open your statement you will need Adobe Reader version 5 or higher. Click here to download. Step 1: Double-click on the attachment. Step 2: Type in your password. Step 3: Click 'OK'. This message has been digitally signed to enable you to verify both its origin and integrity. If the message has been tampered with in any way, a security warning will alert you when you open the mail. To verify the sender 19s digital identity simply click on the red ribbon. Your Password Your password is your account number. To help you avoid confusion if you have multiple current accounts, simply look at the name of the attachment in the email 13 the last four digits are the same as the last four digits in your account number. However, please be sure that you use your full account number when typing in your password(s). Statement Cycle Nedbank will email your statement in accordance with your chosen statement cycle (eg monthly). If we encounter a problem delivering your eStatement 13 eg mailbox is full 13 we will continue trying for up to 24 hours. Thereafter the email will be deemed 18undeliverable 19. If your statements remain undelivered for three consecutive cycles, Nedbank will cease to send you eStatements. To resume the service, simply contact Nedbank on 0860 555 111 and ensure that your eStatements delivery details are up to date. For more information or if you need assistance with your eStatement call 0860 555 111between 07:30 and 18:00 Monday to Friday and 7:30 and 13:00 on Saturdays. eNotes Sign up for Nedbank 19s new active SMS update service ... more... eStatements Choose to have your current account statements delivered straight to your email inbox ... more... Subject: [Spambayes-checkins] spambayes WHAT_IS_NEW.txt,1.42,1.43
Body: Update of /cvsroot/spambayes/spambayes In directory sc8-pr-cvs8.sourceforge.net:/tmp/cvs-serv32054 Modified Files: WHAT_IS_NEW.txt Log Message: Add info about 1.1a4 Index: WHAT_IS_NEW.txt =================================================================== RCS file: /cvsroot/spambayes/spambayes/WHAT_IS_NEW.txt,v retrieving revision 1.42 retrieving revision 1.43 diff -C2 -d -r1.42 -r1.43 *** WHAT_IS_NEW.txt 25 Aug 2006 02:02:12 -0000 1.42 --- WHAT_IS_NEW.txt 7 Jul 2007 06:25:23 -0000 1.43 *************** *** 16,19 **** --- 16,51 ---- is released. + New in 1.1 Alpha 4 + ================== + + -------------------------------------------- + ** Incompatible changes and Transitioning ** + -------------------------------------------- + + Some options that were 'experimental' in 1.1a3 have now been upgraded to + non-experimental, meaning the option names have had their 'x-' prefix removed. + See below for details. + + Otherwise, there should be no incompatible changes since 1.1a3, though users + new to the 1.1 series should pay careful attention to the database changes + introduced in 1.1a2. + + ------------------- + ** Other changes ** + ------------------- + + The previously experimental options 'x-crack-images', 'x-ocr-engine' + and 'x-image-size' have all had their 'x-' prefix removed. 'crack-images' + now defaults to True (meaning you don't need to change anything for it + to be enabled), and ocr-engine defaults to 'gocr'. The Windows binary ships + with the gocr engine, so this should work out-of-the-box for both for Outlook + and POP/IMAP/etc users. + + Image Cracking (ie, using OCR to extract text from images) has been + implemented for the Outlook addin. + + Some localization related issues have been fixed, and a German translation + contributed. + New in 1.1 Alpha 3 ================== *************** *** 91,94 **** --- 123,130 ---- -------------------------------------------- + * NOTE * - this section does not apply to people running SpamBayes on + Windows using the binary installer - only source code installations are + affected. + SpamBayes has changed to use ZODB as the default database backend, rather than dbm (usually bsddb). There are three methods for handling this *************** *** 109,115 **** persistent_use_database:dbm ! o You can convert your existing database files to the new format. ! Windows users will be given the opportunity to do this on installation; ! other users should use the utilities/convert_db.py script to do this. Note that only the token database (containing your training) is converted; the 'messageinfo' database (containing statistics about --- 145,150 ---- persistent_use_database:dbm ! o You can convert your existing database files to the new format using ! the utilities/convert_db.py script. Note that only the token database (containing your training) is converted; the 'messageinfo' database (containing statistics about _______________________________________________ Spambayes-checkins mailing list pnperxdpv-sarswdlp@python.org http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/spambayes-checkins

Subject: Welcome to L.A. on the Record
Body: An L.A. newsletter for avid politics watchers and voters who care Dear reader, Greetings from the nation’s second-largest city, where the future happens first. As your elected officials confront crises and competing visions for the future of Los Angeles, we’ll be here inside City Hall to help you make sense of it all. We want to separate the hot air from the concrete action, translate municipal jargon into plain English and make sure you understand what your local government is doing. Our newsletter is for avid politics watchers, as well as Angelenos who simply care about their city but need some help keeping track of everything happening. We offer a pithy, knowledgeable primer on the week’s events — what mattered, why and what it all means. We’ll be in your inbox every Saturday morning with thoughtful context, dishy tidbits, occasional deep dives and a healthy dose of palace intrigue. Please get in touch with us at LAOnTheRecord@latimes.com. We’d love to hear about what you think will shape these races, why you hated our latest story and where you saw Mayor Karen Bass out for dinner last weekend. Thanks for reading, and look for us in your inbox on Saturdays. Imperial Highway, El Segundo, California, 90245 View Email in Browser
